content:
First observe that any integer approximation \(A_i\) of \(a_i\in[2.56,2.86]\) must be either 2 or 3 (choosing 1 or 4 already gives an error \(\ge1.5\) or \(\ge1.14\), much worse than what we will achieve).  Since 
\[
\sum_{i=1}^7 a_i \;=\; 19,
\]
and \(7\times3=21\), to force the sum of the \(A_i\) to be 19 we must replace exactly two of the \(a_i\) by 2 and the other five by 3.  Which two?

Compute the “2–error’’ \(|2-a_i|\) for each \(i\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
|2-2.56|&=0.56,\\
|2-2.61|&=0.61,\\
|2-2.65|&=0.65,\\
|2-2.71|&=0.71,\dots
\end{aligned}
\]
The two smallest such errors occur at \(a_1=2.56\) and \(a_2=2.61\).  Thus the optimal choice is
\[
A_1=A_2=2,\quad A_3=\cdots=A_7=3,
\]
giving
\[
\max_i|A_i-a_i|=\max\{0.56,\,0.61,\,0.35,\dots\}=0.61.
\]
Hence
\[
M=0.61\quad\Longrightarrow\quad 100M=61.
\]
\boxed{61}

First observe that any integer approximation \(A_i\) of \(a_i\in[2.56,2.86]\) must be either 2 or 3 (choosing 1 or 4 already gives an error \(\ge1.5\) or \(\ge1.14\), much worse than what we will achieve).  Since 
\[
\sum_{i=1}^7 a_i \;=\; 19,
\]
and \(7\times3=21\), to force the sum of the \(A_i\) to be 19 we must replace exactly two of the \(a_i\) by 2 and the other five by 3.  Which two?

Compute the “2–error’’ \(|2-a_i|\) for each \(i\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
|2-2.56|&=0.56,\\
|2-2.61|&=0.61,\\
|2-2.65|&=0.65,\\
|2-2.71|&=0.71,\dots
\end{aligned}
\]
The two smallest such errors occur at \(a_1=2.56\) and \(a_2=2.61\).  Thus the optimal choice is
\[
A_1=A_2=2,\quad A_3=\cdots=A_7=3,
\]
giving
\[
\max_i|A_i-a_i|=\max\{0.56,\,0.61,\,0.35,\dots\}=0.61.
\]
Hence
\[
M=0.61\quad\Longrightarrow\quad 100M=61.
\]
\boxed{61}
